---
title: "03 · Vector Spaces, Linear Independence, Basis"
subtitle: "The abstraction that captures every linear setting at once"
lesson-id: "t5.vector-spaces"
tier: 5
prerequisites: ["t5.matrices", "t4.vectors-2d-3d"]
unlocks: ["t5.eigenvalues"]
applied-domains: ["ai-ml", "research"]
estimated-minutes: 25
---

::: {.preflight-check}
### Pre-flight check {.mt-0}

1. Have you done [01 · Matrices](../01-matrices/index.qmd) and
   [T4 L13 — Vectors](../../tier-4-precalc-calculus/13-vectors-2d-3d/index.qmd)?
2. Comfortable with vector addition and scalar multiplication?
3. Recall the standard basis $\hat{i}, \hat{j}, \hat{k}$ in 3D?
:::

## Touch

A **linear combination** of vectors $v_1, \ldots, v_k$ is $c_1 v_1 +
\cdots + c_k v_k$ for scalars $c_i$. Pick a few vectors and the widget
shows their **span** (all linear combinations).

```{ojs}
viewof spanSel = Inputs.select(["{(1,0)}: a line", "{(1,0), (0,1)}: all of ℝ²", "{(1,0), (2,0)}: a line (collinear)", "{(1,1), (-2,-2)}: a line (collinear)"], {value: "{(1,0), (0,1)}: all of ℝ²", label: "Vectors:"})
```

```{ojs}
{
  let info;
  switch(spanSel) {
    case "{(1,0)}: a line": info = "Span = x-axis. Independent set (only one vector). Dimension 1."; break;
    case "{(1,0), (0,1)}: all of ℝ²": info = "Span = entire plane ℝ². Linearly independent. Forms a basis. Dimension 2."; break;
    case "{(1,0), (2,0)}: a line (collinear)": info = "Span = x-axis only. Linearly DEPENDENT (one is multiple of the other). Doesn't form a basis."; break;
    case "{(1,1), (-2,-2)}: a line (collinear)": info = "Span = the line y = x. Dependent (second is −2× first). Dim 1."; break;
  }
  return html`<div style="margin: 1rem 0; text-align:center;">
    <div style="font-family:monospace; font-size:1.1rem; color:#c2185b;">${info}</div>
  </div>`;
}
```

A **vector space** is a set closed under linear combinations. The
**span** of vectors is everything reachable by linear combination. A
**basis** is a minimal spanning set.

## Wonder

1. Two vectors in the plane: when do they "span" the whole plane?
2. Three vectors in the plane: must one be a combination of the
   others?
3. The **standard basis** $\{\hat{i}, \hat{j}\}$ for $\mathbb{R}^2$ has
   2 vectors. Can a basis have a different number?

## Discover

### Problem 1

A **vector space** $V$ is a set with operations $+$ (addition) and
$\cdot$ (scalar multiplication) satisfying eight axioms (associativity,
commutativity, identity, inverses, distributivity, etc.). Examples:

- $\mathbb{R}^n$ (column vectors).
- The set of all polynomials.
- The set of all continuous functions on $[0, 1]$.

Verify: $\mathbb{R}^2$ is a vector space. (Add a few axioms.)

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$(a + b) + c = a + (b + c)$ ✓ (componentwise associativity).
$a + 0 = a$ ✓ (zero vector identity).
$c(a + b) = ca + cb$ ✓ (distributivity).

These are *familiar* properties for $\mathbb{R}^n$. The abstraction's
power: **the same theorems apply to any vector space**, not just
$\mathbb{R}^n$.
</details>

### Problem 2

The **span** of $\{v_1, v_2\}$ is $\{c_1 v_1 + c_2 v_2 : c_1, c_2 \in
\mathbb{R}\}$.

What's the span of $\{(1, 0), (0, 1)\}$? Of $\{(1, 0), (2, 0)\}$?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
First: $c_1 (1, 0) + c_2 (0, 1) = (c_1, c_2)$ — *every* 2-vector. Span
$= \mathbb{R}^2$.

Second: $c_1 (1, 0) + c_2 (2, 0) = (c_1 + 2 c_2, 0)$ — only x-axis.
Span = $x$-axis (1D line).

The first set "spans more" because the vectors point in *different*
directions; the second set is collinear.
</details>

### Problem 3

Vectors $v_1, \ldots, v_k$ are **linearly independent** iff the only
solution to $c_1 v_1 + \cdots + c_k v_k = 0$ is $c_1 = \cdots = c_k =
0$.

Are $(1, 0)$ and $(0, 1)$ independent? Are $(1, 1)$ and $(2, 2)$?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**$(1, 0)$ and $(0, 1)$:** $c_1 (1, 0) + c_2 (0, 1) = (c_1, c_2) = (0,
0) \Rightarrow c_1 = c_2 = 0$. **Independent.**

**$(1, 1)$ and $(2, 2)$:** $c_1 (1, 1) + c_2 (2, 2) = (c_1 + 2 c_2,
c_1 + 2 c_2) = 0 \Rightarrow c_1 = -2 c_2$. Many non-zero solutions
(e.g., $c_1 = 2, c_2 = -1$). **Dependent.**

Equivalently: dependence means at least one vector is a linear
combination of the others.
</details>

### Problem 4

A **basis** of a vector space $V$ is an *independent* set whose
*span* is all of $V$. Every basis has the same size — the **dimension**
of $V$.

What's the dimension of $\mathbb{R}^2$? Of $\mathbb{R}^3$? Of the
space of degree-≤ 3 polynomials?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\dim \mathbb{R}^2 = 2$ (basis $\{\hat{i}, \hat{j}\}$).
$\dim \mathbb{R}^3 = 3$ (basis $\{\hat{i}, \hat{j}, \hat{k}\}$).
$\dim$(degree-≤ 3 polynomials) = 4 (basis $\{1, x, x^2, x^3\}$).

Dimension is the *number of degrees of freedom* — a deeply important
concept.
</details>

### Problem 5

Show that any 3 vectors in $\mathbb{R}^2$ are linearly **dependent**.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
You have 3 vectors and want to find $(c_1, c_2, c_3) \neq (0, 0, 0)$
with $c_1 v_1 + c_2 v_2 + c_3 v_3 = 0$. That's a homogeneous linear
system in 3 unknowns and 2 equations.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
A homogeneous system with more unknowns than equations *always* has
non-trivial solutions (rank trichotomy from Lesson 02). So 3 vectors
in $\mathbb{R}^2$ are always dependent.

**General fact:** any set of more than $\dim V$ vectors in $V$ is
dependent. Equivalently: a basis is a *maximal* independent set, or
*minimal* spanning set.
</details>

## Name

::: {.lesson-beat}

A **vector space** is a set $V$ with $+$ and scalar multiplication
satisfying the standard axioms.

**Linear combination:** $c_1 v_1 + \cdots + c_k v_k$.

**Span**$(v_1, \ldots, v_k)$ = set of all linear combinations.

**Linearly independent:** the only $c_i$ making $\sum c_i v_i = 0$ are
all zero.

**Basis:** independent + spanning = both. Every basis of $V$ has the
same size: $\dim V$.

**Dimension** of common spaces:

- $\dim \mathbb{R}^n = n$.
- $\dim$(degree-$\le k$ polynomials) $= k + 1$.
- $\dim$(continuous functions on $[0, 1]$) $= \infty$.

**Subspaces:** subsets of $V$ closed under $+$ and scalar
multiplication. Every subspace has its own dimension.

:::

> *Vector spaces unify diverse linear settings;*
> *Span = reachable; independent = no redundancy; basis = both;*
> *Dimension = degrees of freedom = size of any basis.*

## Apply

### Worked example

> Are $v_1 = (1, 2, 3)$, $v_2 = (4, 5, 6)$, $v_3 = (7, 8, 9)$
> linearly independent in $\mathbb{R}^3$?

Solve $c_1 v_1 + c_2 v_2 + c_3 v_3 = 0$, equivalently the system with
matrix $A = [v_1 | v_2 | v_3]^T$. Row-reduce: the third row becomes
zero. So rank 2 < 3. **Dependent.** Indeed, $v_3 - 2 v_2 + v_1 = (7,
8, 9) - (8, 10, 12) + (1, 2, 3) = (0, 0, 0)$.

The set spans only a 2D subspace (a *plane* in $\mathbb{R}^3$).

### Practice — Warm

**W1.** Span of $\{(1, 0)\}$?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
The $x$-axis (a 1D line).
</details>

**W2.** Are $(1, 0, 0)$ and $(2, 0, 0)$ independent?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
No (one is twice the other).
</details>

**W3.** Dim of $\mathbb{R}^5$?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$5$.
</details>

### Practice — Working

**P1.** Are $(1, 1)$ and $(1, -1)$ independent? Do they form a basis
of $\mathbb{R}^2$?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$c_1 (1, 1) + c_2 (1, -1) = (0, 0) \Rightarrow c_1 + c_2 = 0$ and $c_1
- c_2 = 0 \Rightarrow c_1 = c_2 = 0$. **Independent.** Two independent
vectors in $\mathbb{R}^2$ form a basis. Yes.
</details>

**P2.** Dim of the subspace spanned by $\{(1, 1, 0), (0, 1, 1), (1, 2,
1)\}$ in $\mathbb{R}^3$?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Set up matrix, row-reduce. Rank = dimension of span.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
The third is sum of the first two: $(1, 1, 0) + (0, 1, 1) = (1, 2, 1)$.
**Dependent.** Span has dim 2 (a plane).
</details>

**P3.** Find a basis for the plane $x + y + z = 0$ in $\mathbb{R}^3$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$z = -x - y$. Free parameters $x, y$. Express vectors as $(x, y, -x -
y)$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$(x, y, -x - y) = x (1, 0, -1) + y (0, 1, -1)$. **Basis: $\{(1, 0,
-1), (0, 1, -1)\}$.** Plane has dim 2.
</details>

### Practice — Stretch

**S1.** **Polynomial vector space.** The set $P_2$ of polynomials of
degree ≤ 2 is a vector space. Is $\{1, 1 + x, 1 + x + x^2\}$ a basis?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Try to express $1, x, x^2$ (the standard basis) in terms of these.
$1 = 1 \cdot 1$. $x = (1 + x) - 1$. $x^2 = (1 + x + x^2) - (1 + x)$.
**Yes — basis.**

(Alternatively: the matrix of coefficients $\begin{pmatrix} 1 & 1 & 1
\\ 0 & 1 & 1 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{pmatrix}$ is upper triangular with non-
zero diagonal — invertible — so the columns are independent.)

Vector space abstraction lets the *exact same theorems* apply to
polynomials, functions, sequences, matrices, etc.
</details>

**S2.** **Why is dim crucial in ML?** A neural network's *latent
representation* lives in a high-dimensional vector space. A typical
embedding might be 768-dimensional (BERT base), 1024 (GPT-2), or
even 4096+. Why this scale?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**Capacity to disambiguate.** $n$ distinct concepts need at least
$\log_2 n$ bits — but for *graceful interpolation* between concepts,
much higher dimension is helpful. In high dimensions, "almost all"
random vectors are nearly orthogonal, allowing many distinct
features to coexist without interfering.

Modern ML embeddings exploit the geometry of high-dimensional vector
spaces. The vector space abstraction (linear combinations,
independence, basis) is the underlying language. Dimension is a
*resource*: more dimensions = more features representable, but more
parameters to train.

Choosing embedding dimension is one of the central architectural
decisions in modern ML.
</details>

::: {.common-confusion}
### Common confusions

1. **"Span = the vectors themselves."** Span = *all* linear
   combinations, usually a much larger set.
2. **"Independence and orthogonality are the same."** Orthogonal
   implies independent (if non-zero), but not vice versa. $(1, 0)$
   and $(1, 1)$ are independent but not orthogonal.
3. **"A basis is unique."** Many bases exist for any vector space —
   they all have the same size (the dimension), but the vectors
   themselves can differ.
:::

### Self-Assessment

1. Could you state the definition of linear independence?
2. Without scrolling back, what's the dimension of $\mathbb{R}^4$?
3. Did you solve at least 4 of the 5 *Discover* problems and 4 of the 6
   practice problems without peeking past *Hint 1*?

(Answer: $4$.)

- **3 yes:** ready for **[04 · Eigenvalues](../04-eigenvalues/index.qmd)**.
- **2 yes:** redo **S2** (high-dim embeddings).
- **0–1 yes:** re-read *Discover P3 and P5*.

## Connect

### Prerequisites used

- **[01 · Matrices](../01-matrices/index.qmd)** — for the matrix view
  of linear systems.
- **[T4 L13 — Vectors](../../tier-4-precalc-calculus/13-vectors-2d-3d/index.qmd)**

### What this unlocks

- **[04 · Eigenvalues](../04-eigenvalues/index.qmd)** — eigenvectors
  span important subspaces.
- **Tier 6 — Abstract Algebra** — vector spaces are *modules over a
  field*, the gateway to module theory.

### Where this matters

::: {.applied-callout}
**AI / Machine Learning.** Embeddings live in high-dimensional vector
spaces (S2). Dimensionality reduction (PCA, t-SNE, UMAP) finds
lower-dim subspaces preserving important structure.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Mathematical research.** Linear algebra is the foundation of
functional analysis (vector spaces of functions), representation
theory, and quantum mechanics (states are vectors in Hilbert spaces).
:::
